Name: curvature_aware_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing for Deterministic Inference
Short Hypothesis: The curvature of the loss landscape, quantified through Hessian conditioning, can serve as a deterministic proxy for precision allocation in mixed-precision inference, enabling training-free optimization without heuristic control mechanisms.
Related Work: Existing approaches to mixed-precision inference rely on static profiling (e.g., NVIDIA TensorRT) or heuristic control mechanisms (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing by founder_1). These methods often fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions or lack theoretical grounding. Our proposal distinguishes itself by leveraging Hessian conditioning as a deterministic proxy for precision allocation, replacing heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant latency and memory gains but typically relies on static profiling or costly calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time Hessian conditioning. We hypothesize that the local curvature of the loss landscape, quantified through Hessian eigenvalues, serves as a deterministic proxy for quantization sensitivity. Tokens residing in regions with high curvature require higher precision, while those in flatter regions are robust to low-precision computation. CAPR computes these curvature diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized Lanczos algorithms, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate CAPR on WILDS benchmarks (e.g., Camelyon17, iWildCam) to measure throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Compare CAPR against static mixed-precision baselines (e.g., NVIDIA TensorRT) and heuristic control mechanisms (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing).
Quantify the impact of Hessian conditioning on precision allocation by varying the curvature thresholds and measuring the resulting accuracy and latency.
Validate theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds by empirically measuring generalization stability under quantization noise.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the contribution of curvature-aware routing from other components of the framework.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of on-the-fly Hessian conditioning may outweigh the benefits of precision routing.
The effectiveness of CAPR may be limited to specific architectures or tasks where the loss landscape curvature is highly informative.
The randomized Lanczos algorithm may not provide accurate Hessian estimates for very high-dimensional spaces.
The theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds may be too conservative to provide practical guidance for precision allocation.
